blocks:
Ayahs

وَٱلضُّحَىٰ (١)
By the morning brightness, (1)
وَٱلَّيْلِ إِذَا سَجَىٰ (٢)
and by the night when it is calm! (2)
مَا وَدَّعَكَ رَبُّكَ وَمَا قَلَىٰ (٣)
Your Lord has neither forsaken you, nor is He displeased with you, (3)
وَلَلْـَٔاخِرَةُ خَيْرٌ لَّكَ مِنَ ٱلْأُولَىٰ (٤)
and the Hereafter shall be better for you than the world. (4)
وَلَسَوْفَ يُعْطِيكَ رَبُّكَ فَتَرْضَىٰٓ (٥)
Soon your Lord will give you [that with which] you will be pleased. (5)

Content and Virtue of Surah "Ad-Duha"

This Surah, which is among the Meccan Surahs, was revealed—according to some narrations—at a time when the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) was distressed due to a delay and temporary cessation of revelation (Wahy), and the tongues of the enemies had begun to lash out. The Surah descended like a rain of mercy upon the pure heart of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), granting him fresh strength and endurance, and silencing the detractors.

This Surah begins with two oaths, then gives glad tidings to the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) that Allah has never forsaken him.

Next, it promises him that Allah will bestow so much upon him that he will be well-pleased.

In the final stage, it portrays the past life of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) before his eyes, showing how Allah always encompassed him with various forms of His mercy and supported him during the most difficult moments of his life.

Therefore, in the final verses, He commands him (in gratitude for these great divine blessings) to be kind to orphans and the needy, and to proclaim the blessings of God. Regarding the virtue of this Surah, it is sufficient to mention a Hadith narrated from the Noble Prophet (peace be upon him and his family):

"Whoever recites it will be among those with whom Allah is pleased; and it is fitting for Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله) to intercede for him, and he will receive ten good deeds for every orphan and petitioner." [^1] All this virtue belongs to the one who recites it and implements it in practice.

[^1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 503.

It is noteworthy that according to numerous narrations, this Surah and the following Surah (Surah Al-Inshirah) are considered as one Surah. Therefore, since a complete Surah must be recited after Surah Al-Hamd in each unit (Rak'ah) of prayer, these two Surahs must be recited together (a similar point has been mentioned regarding Surahs "Al-Fil" and "Li-Ilaf").

If we carefully reflect on the content of these two Surahs, we see that the connection of their subjects is so strong that one is certainly a continuation of the other, even though they are separated by "Bismillah."

Whether these two Surahs are one from every aspect, or specifically for the purpose of prayer they should be treated as one, is a discussion whose details should be studied in books of jurisprudence (the section on prayer recitation). However, in any case, the consensus of scholars is that in the recitation of prayer, one cannot suffice with only one of these two.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ وَ الضُّحى (1) وَ اللَّيْلِ إِذا سَجى (2) ما وَدَّعَكَ رَبُّكَ وَ ما قَلى (3) وَ لَلْآخِرَةُ خَيْرٌ لَكَ مِنَ الْأُولى (4) وَ لَسَوْفَ يُعْطِيكَ رَبُّكَ فَتَرْضى (5)

Occasion of Revelation

Regarding the occasion of revelation of this Surah, many narrations have been cited, the clearest of which is the following:

Ibn Abbas says: Fifteen days passed and revelation did not descend upon the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family). The polytheists said: "The Lord of Muhammad has forsaken him and hated him; if he were truthful that his mission is from God, revelation should have descended upon him regularly." At this point, the above Surah was revealed (and responded to their words).

It is noteworthy that according to a Hadith, when this Surah was revealed, the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) said to Gabriel: "You delayed while I was intensely longing for you." Gabriel replied: "و انا كنت اشد اليك شوقا" (And I was even more longing for you, but I am a commissioned servant and I do not descend except by the command of my Lord)! In another Hadith, it is mentioned that a group of Jews came to the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his family) and asked about the story of "Dhul-Qarnayn," the "People of the Cave," and the creation of the "Soul." The Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) said, "I will inform you tomorrow," but he did not say "Insha'Allah" (If Allah wills). This caused the divine revelation to be cut off from him for some days, and the tongues of the enemies opened with taunts, making the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) sad. The Surah was revealed to console the Prophet's heart (however, this occasion of revelation seems unlikely because the contact of the Jews with the Noble Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) and such questions usually occurred in Medina, not Mecca).

In some narrations, it is also mentioned that a group of Muslims asked: "O Messenger of Allah! Why does revelation not descend upon you?" He replied: "How can revelation descend upon me while you do not clean your finger joints and do not clip your nails!?" [^1] Regarding the duration of the cessation of revelation, narrations vary: some say twelve days, some fifteen, some nineteen, some twenty-five, and some have even cited forty days. In one narration, it is cited as only two or three days and nights.

Commentary

He will give you so much that you will be satisfied!

At the beginning of this Surah, we encounter two oaths: an oath by "Light" and an oath by "Darkness." He says: "By the morning brightness when the sun rises and covers everywhere" (وَ الضُّحى).

"And by the night when it settles and covers everywhere in tranquility" (وَ اللَّيْلِ إِذا سَجى). "Duha" means the early part of the day when the sun rises high in the sky and its light dominates everywhere. This is actually the best time of the day, and as some describe it, it is like the season of youth; in summer the air is not yet hot, and in winter the cold has been broken, and the human soul and spirit are ready for any activity at this time.

"Saja" is derived from the root "Sajw," which originally means "stillness and tranquility." It has also been used to mean "covering" and "becoming dark," which is why when a deceased person is wrapped in a shroud, they are called "Musajja." However, here it conveys the original meaning, which is stillness and tranquility. For this reason, nights when the wind does not blow are called "Laylatun Sajiyah" (a calm night), and a sea free from storms and roaring waves is called "Bahrun Saj" (a calm sea).

In any case, what is important about the night is the very tranquility that reigns over it, which naturally immerses the human nerves and spirit in peace and prepares them for the efforts of tomorrow and the days to follow. From this perspective, it is a very important blessing worthy of being sworn by.

There is a close resemblance and relationship between these two oaths and the content of the verse: the day is like the descent of the light of revelation upon the pure heart of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), and the night is like the temporary cessation of revelation, which is also necessary at certain intervals.

Following these two great oaths, He proceeds to the result and the response to the oath, saying: "Your Lord has neither forsaken you, nor has He become hateful [of you]" (ما وَدَّعَكَ رَبُّكَ وَ ما قَلى).

"Wadda'a" is from the root "Tawdi'" meaning to leave or bid farewell. "Qala" is from the root "Qila" meaning intensity of hatred and enmity; it also comes from the root "Qalw" meaning to throw. Raghib believes both return to one meaning, for one who is the object of enmity is as if the heart throws them away and does not accept them. [^1] In any case, this expression is a comfort and consolation for the person of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) so he may know that if there is sometimes a delay in the descent of revelation, it is based on wisdom known to Allah, and it is never a reason—as the enemies claimed—that Allah has become angry with him or intends to leave him. He is always subject to the special grace and favors of Allah and is constantly under His special protection.

Then He adds: "And surely the Hereafter is better for you than the first [life]" (وَ لَلْآخِرَةُ خَيْرٌ لَكَ مِنَ الْأُولى). You are subject to His favors in this world, and in the Hereafter even more and better. You will neither be subject to the wrath of the Lord in the short term, nor in the long term. In short, you are dear in this world and the Hereafter—dear in this world and even dearer in the Hereafter.

Some commentators have considered "Akhirah" (the last) and "Ula" (the first) as references to the beginning and end of the Prophet's (peace be upon him and his family) life, saying the meaning is that you will be more successful and victorious in the future of your life. This refers to the spread of Islam, the repeated victories of Muslims over enemies, their conquests in battles, the blossoming of the seedling of Monotheism, and the eradication of the traces of polytheism and idolatry. Combining these two interpretations is also possible.

[^1]: This root appears both as "Naqis-i Ya'i" and "Naqis-i Wawi." In the first case, it means hatred and enmity, and in the second case, it means throwing and rejecting. As mentioned above, both meanings return to one root.

In the last verse under discussion, He gives him the greatest glad tidings, adding: "And your Lord will soon give you so much that you will be well-pleased" (وَ لَسَوْفَ يُعْطِيكَ رَبُّكَ فَتَرْضى).

This is the highest honor and respect from the Lord toward His special servant Muhammad (peace be upon him and his family), saying: We will grant you so much that you will be satisfied. In this world, you will be victorious over enemies and your religion will encompass the world, and in the Hereafter, you will also be subject to the greatest gifts.

Undoubtedly, the Noble Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), as the Seal of the Prophets and the leader of humanity, finds his satisfaction not only in his own salvation but rather he becomes satisfied and pleased when his intercession for his Ummah is also accepted. For this reason, it is mentioned in narrations that this verse is the most hopeful verse of the Holy Quran and proof of the acceptance of his intercession.

In a Hadith from Imam Baqir (عليه السلام), from his father Zayn al-Abidin (عليه السلام), from his uncle Muhammad ibn al-Hanafiyyah, from his father the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام), we read: The Messenger of Allah said: "On the Day of Resurrection, I will stand in the station of intercession and intercede for the sinners so much that Allah will say: 'Are you satisfied, O Muhammad?!' I will say: 'I am satisfied, I am satisfied!'" Then the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام) turned to a group of people from Kufa and added: "You believe the most hopeful verse of the Quran is: 'Say, O My servants who have transgressed against themselves, do not despair of the mercy of Allah...'"

They said: "Yes, that is what we say." He said: "But we, the Ahl al-Bayt, say the most hopeful verse of the Quran is: 'And your Lord will soon give you so much that you will be well-pleased.'" [^1]

It goes without saying that the intercession of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) has conditions; neither does he intercede for everyone, nor can every sinner have such an expectation (please study the details of this discussion in Volume 1 under verse 48 of Surah Al-Baqarah).

In another Hadith from Imam Sadiq (عليه السلام), we read: "The Messenger of Allah entered the house of Fatimah (عليه السلام) while his daughter was wearing a coarse garment of camel hair, grinding with one hand and nursing her child with the other. Tears appeared in the Prophet's (peace be upon him and his family) eyes; he said: 'My daughter! Endure the bitterness of this world for the sweetness of the Hereafter, for Allah has revealed to me: And your Lord will soon give you so much that you will be well-pleased.'" [^2]

Key Point: The Philosophy of the Cessation of Revelation

From the totality of the above verses, it becomes clearly evident that whatever the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) has is from Allah; he has no independent choice even in the descent of revelation. Whenever Allah wills, revelation is cut off, and whenever He wills, it is established. Perhaps the cessation of revelation was for this very purpose: to be an answer to those who demanded arbitrary miracles from the Noble Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) according to their own desires, or suggested that he change such-and-all a ruling or verse, and he would say: "I have no choice of my own in these matters" (as stated in verse 15 of Surah Yunus).

[^1]: "Abu al-Futuh Razi," Vol. 12, p. 110. [^2]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 505.

Ayahs

أَلَمْ يَجِدْكَ يَتِيمًا فَـَٔاوَىٰ (٦)
Did He not find you an orphan, and shelter you? (6)
وَوَجَدَكَ ضَآلًّا فَهَدَىٰ (٧)
Did He not find you astray, and guide you? (7)
وَوَجَدَكَ عَآئِلًا فَأَغْنَىٰ (٨)
Did He not find you needy, and enrich you? (8)
فَأَمَّا ٱلْيَتِيمَ فَلَا تَقْهَرْ (٩)
So, as for the orphan, do not oppress him; (9)
وَأَمَّا ٱلسَّآئِلَ فَلَا تَنْهَرْ (١٠)
and as for the beggar, do not chide him; (10)
وَأَمَّا بِنِعْمَةِ رَبِّكَ فَحَدِّثْ (١١)
and as for your Lord’s blessing, proclaim it! (11)

Commentary

In gratitude for all these blessings that God has bestowed upon you...

As we have stated, the objective of this Surah is to solace and comfort the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) and to recount the Divine favors toward him. Therefore, continuing the previous verses that spoke of this theme, the verses under discussion first mention three specific Divine gifts granted to the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), and subsequently issue three important commands to him in this regard.

He says: "Did He not find you an orphan and give you shelter?" (أَ لَمْ يَجِدْكَ يَتِيماً فآوى).

You were in your mother's womb when your father, Abdullah, passed away; I raised you in the embrace of your grandfather, "Abdul-Muttalib" (the chief of Mecca).

You were six years old when your mother passed away, and you became alone in that regard as well, but I increased the love and affection for you in the heart of "Abdul-Muttalib."

You were eight years old when your grandfather "Abdul-Muttalib" passed away; I appointed your uncle "Abu Talib" to serve and support you, so that he might embrace and protect you like his own sweet soul.

Yes, you were an orphan, and I gave you shelter.

Some commentators have suggested other meanings for this verse that do not align with its literal appearance. Among them is that "orphan" (Yatim) refers to someone who has no peer or equal in nobility and virtue, just as a unique pearl is called "Durr-e-Yatim" (the orphan pearl). According to this meaning, the sentence would be: "Allah found you peerless in nobility and merit, thus He chose you and granted you the station of Prophethood."

Another interpretation is: "You yourself were once an orphan, but eventually became the refuge of orphans and the leader of humanity."

Undoubtedly, the first meaning is more appropriate in every respect and more harmonious with the literal text of the verse.

Then, mentioning the second blessing, He says: "And He found you wandering and guided you" (وَ وَجَدَكَ ضَالًّا فَهَدى).

Yes, you were never aware of Prophethood and the Message, and We cast this light into your heart so that you might guide humanity by it, as He says elsewhere:

"You did not know what the Book was, nor what was faith (you were not aware of the contents of the Quran and Islam before the descent of revelation), but We made it a light by which We guide whomsoever We will of Our servants" (Ash-Shura: 52) (ما كُنْتَ تَدْرِي مَا الْكِتابُ وَ لَا الْإِيمانُ وَ لكِنْ جَعَلْناهُ نُوراً نَهْدِي بِهِ مَنْ نَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِنا).

It is clear that before reaching the station of Prophethood and the Message, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) lacked this Divine grace; Allah took his hand, guided him, and placed him in this station, as we read in Surah Yusuf, verse 3:

"We relate to you the best of stories through Our revelation of this Quran to you, though before it you were among the unaware" (نَحْنُ نَقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ أَحْسَنَ الْقَصَصِ بِما أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ هذَا الْقُرْآنَ وَ إِنْ كُنْتَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِ لَمِنَ الْغافِلِينَ).

Certainly, if Divine guidance and unseen aids had not taken the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) hand, he would never have found the way to the ultimate destination.

Therefore, the meaning of "wandering" (dalalat) here is not the negation of faith, monotheism, purity, or piety; rather, by the context of the verses mentioned above, it refers to the lack of awareness regarding the secrets of Prophethood and the laws of Islam, and the lack of familiarity with these truths—as many commentators have stated. However, after the appointment (Bi'thah), with the help of the Lord, he became cognizant of all these matters and attained guidance (reflect deeply).

In verse 282 of Surah Al-Baqarah, while mentioning the philosophy behind the plurality of witnesses in the matter of writing debt contracts, He says: "So that if one of them wanders (forgets), the other may remind her" (أَنْ تَضِلَّ إِحْداهُما فَتُذَكِّرَ إِحْداهُمَا الْأُخْرى).

In this verse, "dalalat" is used only in the sense of forgetting, by the context of the phrase "so she may remind."

Several other interpretations have also been mentioned for this verse here, including:

It means that you were unknown and without fame, and Allah granted you such unique gifts that you became known everywhere.

Or: You were lost several times during your childhood (once in the valleys of Mecca while under the protection of Abdul-Muttalib; another time when your foster mother "Halimah Sa'diyyah" was bringing you back to Mecca to return you to Abdul-Muttalib after weaning, and you were lost on the way; and a third time when you were with your uncle "Abu Talib" in a caravan going toward Syria and lost the way on a dark and gloomy night), and Allah guided you in all these instances and returned you to the loving embrace of your grandfather or uncle.

It is noteworthy that the word "Dall" (ضال) linguistically has two meanings: "lost" and "wandering/misguided." For example, it is said: "Wisdom is the lost property (dallah) of the believer" (الحكمة ضالة المؤمن).

For this reason, it is also used in the sense of "hidden" or "absent." Thus, in Surah As-Sajdah, verse 10, we read that the deniers of the Resurrection said: "When we are hidden (lost) in the earth, shall we then be in a new creation?" (أَ إِذا ضَلَلْنا فِي الْأَرْضِ أَ إِنَّا لَفِي خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ).

If "Dall" in the verse under discussion means "lost," no problem arises. If it means "wandering," it refers to not having access to the path of Prophethood and the Message before the appointment; or in other words, the Prophet had nothing of his own essence—whatever he had was from Allah. Therefore, in both cases, no problem arises.

Then, stating the third blessing, He says: "And He found you poor and made you rich and self-sufficient" (وَ وَجَدَكَ عائِلًا فَأَغْنى) [^1]. He drew the attention of "Khadijah," that sincere and faithful woman, toward you so that she might place her vast wealth at the disposal of you and your great goals; and after the advent of Islam, He granted you abundant spoils in wars such that you became self-sufficient in achieving your great objectives.

In a narration from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida (عليه السلام), we read that he said in the interpretation of these verses:

"Did He not find you an orphan (Yatim)—meaning unique with no peer among the creatures—and then gave people shelter through you? And He found you wandering (lost)—meaning unknown among a people who did not recognize your merit—and He guided them to you? And He found you poor (A'ilan)—supporting peoples with knowledge—and He made them rich through you" [^2] (ا لم يجدك يتيما فاوى قال: فردا لا مثل لك فى المخلوقين، فاوى الناس اليك، و وجدك ضالا اى ضالة فى قوم لا يعرفون فضلك فهداهم اليك، و وجدك عائلا تعول اقواما بالعلم فاغناهم بك).

[^1]: "A'il" (عائل) originally means a person with a family to support, even if they are wealthy, but this word is also used to mean "poor," and in the verse under discussion, it refers to this meaning. From the words of "Raghib," it is understood that if "Aal" (عال) is from the root with 'ya', it means "poor," and if it is from the root with 'waw', it means having a large family (though it is not unlikely that these two are concomitants of each other).

[^2]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 506.

Of course, this Hadith refers to the inner depths (batn) of the verse; otherwise, the literal meaning of the verses is what was stated above.

Lest it be imagined that mentioning these matters—which are in the literal sense of the verse—diminishes the lofty status of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), or is a negative description by the Lord regarding him; rather, this is in truth an expression of Divine favors and His honoring and respecting this great Prophet. When the Beloved speaks of His favors toward the devoted lover, it is the essence of grace and affection and evidence of His special care. For this reason, upon hearing these words from the Beloved, the spirit is renewed, the soul finds purity, and the heart is drowned in tranquility and peace.

In the subsequent verses, as a conclusion from the preceding verses, He gives three highly important commands to the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله). Although the addressee is the person of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله), they certainly include everyone. First, He says: "Therefore, do not treat the orphan with harshness" (فَأَمَّا الْيَتِيمَ فَلا تَقْهَرْ).

"Taqhar" (تقهر) is from the root "Qahr" (قهر), which according to "Raghib" in "Al-Mufradat" means "subjugation accompanied by humiliation," but it is also used for each of these two meanings separately. Here, "humiliation" is the most appropriate meaning.

This shows that regarding orphans, although the matter of feeding and spending is important, even more important is comforting them, showing affection, and filling their emotional voids. Thus, in a famous Hadith, we read that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) said:

"Whoever strokes the head of an orphan out of affection, for every hair his hand passes over, he shall have a light on the Day of Resurrection" [^1] (من مسح على رأس يتيم كان له بكل شعرة تمر على يده نور يوم القيامة). It is as if Allah is saying to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله): "You too were an orphan and have felt the pain of orphanhood; now, with all your heart and soul, look after the orphans and quench their thirsty spirits with love."

And in the next verse, addressing the second command, He says: "And do not repulse the petitioner" (وَ أَمَّا السَّائِلَ فَلا تَنْهَرْ).

"La tanhar" (لا تنهر) is from the root "Nahr" (نهر), meaning "to drive away with harshness." It is not unlikely that its root is the same as "Nahr" meaning a flowing river, because it also drives the water with force! Regarding who is meant by "petitioner" (Sa'il) here, several interpretations exist:

First, it refers to those who have questions regarding scientific, ideological, and religious matters, by the context that this command is a branch of what appeared in the previous verses: "And He found you wandering and guided you." Since Allah guided you, in gratitude for this Divine guidance, you too should strive to guide the needy and never drive away anyone requesting guidance.

Second, it refers to those who suffer from material poverty and come to you; you must use whatever is in your power and not make them lose hope or drive them away.

Third, it refers to both intellectual poverty and material poverty. It commands to give a positive response to the requests of petitioners in every area. This meaning is consistent both with the Divine guidance toward the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the care provided to him when he was an orphan.

[^1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 506.

Surprisingly, some commentators, to prove that "petitioner" here refers only to one who asks about scientific matters, have said that the term "Sa'il" in the Holy Quran has never been used to mean a petitioner for wealth [^1], whereas it has been used repeatedly in the Quran with this meaning. For instance, in Surah Adh-Dhariyat, verse 19, we read: "And in their wealth was a right for the petitioner and the deprived" (وَ فِي أَمْوالِهِمْ حَقٌّ لِلسَّائِلِ وَ الْمَحْرُومِ). The same meaning appears in Surah Al-Ma'arij, verse 25, and Al-Baqarah, verse 177.

Finally, in the third and last command, He says: "And as for the blessing of your Lord, proclaim it" (وَ أَمَّا بِنِعْمَةِ رَبِّكَ فَحَدِّثْ).

Proclaiming a blessing is sometimes done with the tongue and expressions that indicate the ultimate gratitude and praise—not pride or seeking superiority—and sometimes it is done through action, by spending and giving from it in the way of Allah; a giving that shows Allah has granted him abundant blessings.

It is the tradition of generous and noble individuals that when a blessing comes to them, they proclaim it and offer thanks to God, and their actions also serve as a confirmation and emphasis of this truth. In contrast are the base-minded misers who constantly complain, and even if you gave them the whole world, they would insist on concealing the blessings; their appearance is impoverished, their words are mixed with sighs and lamentations, and their actions also express poverty! This is while it has been narrated from the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) that he said:

"Indeed, when Allah Almighty bestows a blessing upon a servant, He loves to see the effect of the blessing upon him" [^1] (ان اللَّه تعالى اذا انعم على عبد نعمة يحب ان يرى اثر النعمة عليه).

[^1]: "Tafsir Abduh," Juz' Amma, p. 113.

Therefore, the sum of the verse's meaning is: "In gratitude for the fact that you were poor and Allah made you self-sufficient, you too manifest the effects of the blessing, and proclaim this Divine gift through speech and action."

However, some commentators have said that "blessing" here refers only to spiritual blessings, including Prophethood or the Holy Quran, which the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was duty-bound to convey, and this is the meaning of proclaiming the blessing.

It is also possible that it includes all spiritual and material blessings.

Thus, in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), we read that he said the meaning of the verse is:

"Proclaim what Allah has given you, how He has favored you, what He has provided for you, how He has been good to you, and how He has guided you" [^2] (حدث بما اعطاك اللَّه، و فضلك، و رزقك، و احسن اليك و هداك).

And finally, in another Hadith from the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) as a general instruction, we read:

"Whoever is given good and its effect is not seen upon him, is called a hater of Allah, an enemy to the blessings of Allah!" [^3] (من اعطى خيرا فلم ير عليه، سمى بغيض اللَّه، معاديا لنعم اللَّه!).

We conclude this discussion with another Hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام), who said:

"Indeed, Allah is beautiful and He loves beauty! And He also loves to see the effect of the blessing upon His servant" [^1] (ان اللَّه جميل يحب الجمال، و يحب ان يرى اثر النعمة على عبده).

[^1]: "Nahj al-Fasahah," Hadith 683. [^2]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 507. [^3]: "Tafsir al-Qurtubi," Vol. 10, p. 7192. A similar meaning is found in "Al-Kafi," Vol. 6, Book of Attire and Adornment, Hadith 2.

[^1]: "Furu' al-Kafi," Vol. 6, p. 438, Hadith.

Key Points

1- Leadership Born from Midst of Pain and Suffering!

The above verses, while explaining Allah's blessings to the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), also reflect the point that he was an orphan at the beginning of childhood, lived in difficult material conditions, was acquainted with pain and suffering, and rose from the midst of hardships—and it must be so.

A Divine and humane leader must taste the bitterness of life, personally touch the discomforts, and feel the hardships with his entire being, so that he can have a correct assessment of the deprived classes of society and be aware of the state of people who are submerged in pain and suffering.

He must lose his father in childhood to be aware of the pain of orphan children; he must remain hungry for days and sleep hungry at night to understand the pain of the hungry with his whole being.

Therefore, whenever he saw an orphan, tears would flow from his blessed eyes; he would sit the child on his lap, comfort him, and embrace him like his own sweet soul.

He must have well understood the cultural poverty of society so that he might honor those who come to him to acquire knowledge and welcome them with open arms.

Not only the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), but perhaps all prophets were nurtured by suffering and deprivation; and not only prophets, but all true and successful leaders were such, and must be such.

How can one who was raised in luxury and comfort, lived in magnificent palaces, and had whatever he wanted at his disposal at any time, understand the pain of the deprived, visualize the scene of the homes of the poor and the dwellings of orphans, and rush to their aid?!

In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), we read:

"Allah never sent a prophet except that He made him tend sheep, to teach him thereby the tending of people" [^1] (ما بعث اللَّه نبيا قط حتى يسترعيه الغنم يعلمه بذلك رعية الناس). That is, they both suffered and experienced patience and endurance in the face of a creature with little intellect, and they learned the great lessons of Monotheism and Gnosis in the mountains, deserts, and the embrace of nature.

In another Hadith, it is mentioned that "Musa ibn 'Imran" asked his Lord: "For what reason did I reach this station?" The address came: "Do you remember the day a lamb fled from your flock? You followed it and caught it, and said to it: 'O animal! Why did you tire yourself?' Then you placed it on your shoulders and returned it to the flock. For this reason, I made you the guardian of the creation" (this extraordinary endurance and patience of yours toward an animal is evidence of your great spiritual power, and thus you are worthy of that great station).

[^1]: "Bihar al-Anwar," Vol. 11, p. 64, Hadith 7.

2- Comforting Orphans

The existence of orphan children who have lost their fathers in childhood is inevitable in any society. These children must be supported from various aspects.

Emotionally, they have deficiencies; if the void of their existence is not filled in this regard, they grow up as unhealthy children, and in many cases, as cold-hearted, criminal, and dangerous individuals. Furthermore, human emotions require that they, like other children of society, be supported and attended to by the public. Beyond all this, people should be reassured about the future of their own children who might find themselves in such conditions.

"Orphans" in many cases possess property that must be preserved for their future with precision and trustworthiness, and in many cases, they lack financial means and must be attended to in this regard as well. Others, like compassionate fathers and mothers, must remove the pain of orphanhood from their souls and wipe the dust of loneliness from their faces.

Therefore, in the verses of the Holy Quran and Islamic narrations, great emphasis has been placed on this issue, which has both an ethical dimension and a social and human dimension.

This Hadith from the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) is famous:

"Indeed, when an orphan cries, the Throne of the All-Merciful trembles at his crying!" Allah says to His angels: "O My angels! Who has made this orphan cry, whose father is hidden in the dust?" The angels say: "O God! You are more knowing." Allah says: "O My angels! I take you as witnesses that whoever silences his crying and gladdens his heart, I shall gladden him on the Day of Resurrection" [^1].

Higher than this, we read another Hadith from the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله):

"Whenever an orphan cries, his tears fall into the palm of the All-Merciful (Ar-Rahman)"! [^2]

Again, in another Hadith from him, it is narrated that he said:

"I and the guardian of an orphan will be like these two in Paradise—provided he fears Allah, the Mighty and Majestic"—then he pointed to his index finger and middle finger [^3].

The importance of this subject is so great that the Commander of the Faithful, Ali, in his famous will, placed attention to orphans alongside attention to prayer and the Quran, saying:

"Fear Allah, fear Allah regarding orphans; do not let them go hungry one day and full the next, and let them not be lost while you are present" [^4] (اللَّه اللَّه فى الايتام فلا تغبوا افواههم و لا يضيعوا بحضرتكم).

In a Hadith from one of the companions of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), it is said: "We were sitting with the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) when a young boy came to him and said: 'I am an orphan boy, and I have an orphan sister and a widowed mother. Feed us from what Allah has fed you, so that Allah may grant you from what is with Him until you are satisfied!'"

The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said: "How beautifully you spoke, O boy!" Then he turned to Bilal and said: "Go and bring what is with us." Bilal brought twenty-one dates. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said: "Seven for you, seven for your sister, and seven for your mother."

"Mu'adh ibn Jabal" stood up, stroked the orphan boy's head, and said: "May Allah compensate your orphanhood and make you a righteous successor for your father" (the orphan boy was from the children of the Muhajirun).

The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) turned to Mu'adh and asked: "What was your motive for this?" He replied: "It was love and mercy."

He said: "Whoever among you takes charge of an orphan and fulfills his right, and strokes the head of the orphan, Allah writes for him a good deed for every hair, and erases a sin for every hair, and grants him a degree for every hair" [^1].

Of course, in vast societies like today's, Muslims must not suffice with individual actions in this field; rather, they must concentrate their forces and place orphans under the coverage of calculated economic, cultural, and educational programs, and build them into capable individuals for the Islamic society. This important matter requires public cooperation.

[^1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 506. [^2]: "Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi," Vol. 31, p. 219. [^3]: "Nur al-Thaqalayn," Vol. 5, p. 597, Hadith 23. [^4]: "Nahj al-Balagha," Letters section, Letter 47. [^1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 506.

3- Proclaiming Blessings

The command given in the above verses in this regard—provided it is done as Divine gratitude and thanks, and not as pride or seeking superiority—besides perfecting man in the station of servitude to the Lord and having positive social effects, also has a calming effect on the soul and spirit of the person himself.

Reminding oneself of Allah's blessings causes a person to feel deficiencies less, not to complain about illnesses because he is grateful for the health of his other members, and not to wail and lament over losing something because he proclaims his remaining resources.

Such individuals do not fall into despair, hopelessness, anxiety, and distress during the hardships and storms of life; they have a calm spirit and a reassured heart, and their power to struggle against problems is great.

O Allah! Your blessings are more than can be proclaimed; do not strip them from us, and increase them by Your generosity. O Lord! We are drowned in Your favor in this world; we expect to be so in the Hereafter as well. O God! Grant us the success to always be supporters of the deprived and protectors of the rights of orphans.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah Ad-Duha.
